question: Which of the following sets is countably infinite?
options: The set of all integers (Z)
The set of all real numbers between 0 and 1
The power set of the natural numbers (P(N))
The set of all functions from R to R
explanation: The set of all integers (Z) can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with the natural numbers (N), which is the definition of a countably infinite set.

question: What is the cardinality of the set of all rational numbers (Q)?
options: ℵ₀ (aleph-null)
c (the cardinality of the continuum)
ℵ₁
It is finite
explanation: Despite being dense on the real number line, the set of rational numbers is countably infinite (ℵ₀). This can be proven by arranging the rationals in a grid and using a diagonal counting argument.

question: Cantor's theorem states that for any set S, the cardinality of its power set P(S) is:
options: Strictly greater than the cardinality of S
Equal to the cardinality of S
Less than or equal to the cardinality of S
Always equal to the cardinality of the continuum
explanation: Cantor's theorem is a fundamental result which proves that for any set S, there is no surjection from S onto P(S). Therefore, the cardinality of P(S) is strictly greater than the cardinality of S itself.

question: Which of the following sets has the same cardinality as the set of all real numbers (R)?
options: The power set of the natural numbers, P(N)
The set of all continuous functions from R to R
The set of all natural numbers (N)
The set of all integers (Z)
explanation: The cardinality of R is known as 'c' (the continuum). It can be proven that |R| = |P(N)|, meaning the set of all subsets of the natural numbers has the same cardinality as the real numbers.

question: The Continuum Hypothesis proposes a specific relationship between which two cardinal numbers?
options: It states that there is no set whose cardinality is strictly between ℵ₀ and c.
It states that ℵ₁ is equal to ℵ₀.
It states that c is equal to ℵ₀.
It states that ℵ₁ is strictly less than c.
explanation: The Continuum Hypothesis asserts that the cardinality of the continuum (c) is the next largest cardinal number after ℵ₀ (the cardinality of the naturals), i.e., c = ℵ₁. This means there is no set with cardinality strictly between that of the natural numbers and the real numbers.
